lines.text: Why am I — because it's mine? Because I bought it with my own money? Because the structural report says it needs four million dollars in repairs that nobody is volunteering to pay?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Everyone acts like I'm some monster for wanting to tear down a building. You know what they don't tell you in the brochure? The roof leaks. The foundation has cracks. The electrical system is a fire hazard that would make any inspector weep. The plumbing dates back to an era when 'indoor plumbing' was considered cutting-edge technology.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That doesn't mean—

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I know. I know what it 'means.' It means history. Culture. Heritage. I've heard the speech. Edwin's given it to me forty-seven times. Vivian's given it to me in two languages. I get it. But sentiment doesn't pay for earthquake retrofitting. Nostalgia doesn't fix a gas line from 1924.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I'm not the villain here, Drew. I'm just the guy holding the bill.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Enemies? Ha. Take a number. Stand in line.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Vivian looks at me like I'm something she found on the bottom of her shoe. Edwin talks about me the way people talk about war criminals. Stella — actually, Stella is professional about it, I'll give her that. She just quietly hates me with impeccable time management.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Diego is the only one who doesn't seem to actively despise me, which is probably because he's too busy scribbling in his notebook to notice I exist. I caught him taking notes during my meeting with the contractor. Notes! Like I'm a character in his little play.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You don't seem very concerned about being surrounded by people who dislike you.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I'm a real estate developer, Ms. Drew. If I worried about being liked, I'd have chosen a different career. Maybe something universally beloved, like dentistry or parking enforcement.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Finally. Go. Investigate. Find whoever poisoned me so I can sue them. And close the door on your way out — there's a draft from the hallway that's been bothering me for forty-five minutes and I refuse to get up because my legs still feel like they're made of pudding.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy, ma chère. You have that look again — the furrow. Go, go. The theater will keep its secrets whether I'm talking or not. I'll be here, as always.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And remember — crackers in the concession stand. Only slightly stale.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I've told you everything I know, Nancy. Everything I'm — well. Everything I can. If you need architectural details or historical records, I'm your man. Otherwise, I should get back to my notes. Chapter twelve won't revise itself.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You have a look in your eye that suggests you already know more than I'd like. That's... unsettling. In a professional sense.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Drew. I've told you everything. I've told you more than everything. I've told you things I haven't told my therapist, and she charges $200 an hour.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Go solve the mystery. I've got a fly system to rebalance and approximately zero patience left for questions.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Nancy! I'd love to chat but I'm in a really good writing flow right now. The words are just — *chef's kiss* — they're happening. If I stop now, I'll lose the thread and spend three hours staring at a blinking cursor.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: But hey — when you solve this whole thing? I want details. For the play. Strictly for artistic purposes.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: We're done, Drew. I've answered your questions. I've shown you my office. I've been more cooperative than any man who was recently poisoned should have to be.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: The demolition is in seventy-two hours. Either find the poisoner by then or get out of my way. Preferably both.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I pick up the receiver and listen. Just a dial tone. I've called everyone I can think of — Bess, George, Dad, Ned, even the Historical Society. Time to put down the phone and put the pieces together myself.
